results.title: AI Agents in 2025: Expectations vs. Reality | IBM
excerpts:
Section Title: AI agents in 2025: Expectations vs. reality > What are AI agents?
Content:
Current offerings are still in the early stages of approaching this idea. “What’s commonly referred to as ‘agents’ in the market is the addition of rudimentary planning and tool-calling (sometimes called function calling) capabilities to LLMs,” says Ashoori. “These enable the LLM to break down complex tasks into smaller steps that the LLM can perform.”
Hay is optimistic that more robust agents are on the way: “You wouldn’t need any further progression in models today to build [future AI agents](https://www.ibm.com/think/insights/ai-agents-evolve-rapidly) ,” he says. 
With that out of the way, what’s the conversation about agents over the coming year, and how much of it can we take seriously? 
AI agents
 ... 
Section Title: AI agents in 2025: Expectations vs. reality > ... > 2025 as the year of agentic exploration
Content:
“IBM and Morning Consult did a survey of 1,000 developers who are building AI applications for enterprise, and 99% of them said they are exploring or developing AI agents,” explains Ashoori. “So yes, the answer is that 2025 is going to be the year of the agent.” However, that declaration is not without nuance. 
After establishing the current market conception of agents as LLMs with function calling, Ashoori draws a distinction between that idea and truly autonomous agents. “The true definition [of an AI agent] is an intelligent entity with reasoning and planning capabilities that can autonomously take action. Those reasoning and planning capabilities are up for discussion. It depends on how you define that.” 
 ...

results.title: [2505.16067] How Memory Management Impacts LLM Agents: An Empirical Study of Experience-Following Behavior
excerpts:
Section Title: Title: How Memory Management Impacts LLM Agents: An Empirical Study of Experience-Following Behavior
Content:
Abstract: Memory is a critical component in large language model (LLM)-based agents, enabling them to store and retrieve past executions to improve task performance over time. In this paper, we conduct an empirical study on how memory management choices impact the LLM agents' behavior, especially their long-term performance. Specifically, we focus on two fundamental memory management operations that are widely used by many agent frameworks-memory addition and deletion-to systematically study their impact on the agent behavior. Through our quantitative analysis, we find that LLM agents display an experience-following property: high similarity between a task input and the input in a retrieved memory record often results in highly similar agent outputs.
 ...

results.title: Why Memory Is the Real Bottleneck for AI Agents — And What It Means for Multi-Agent Swarms | by Richard Chen | Feb, 2026 | Medium
excerpts:
Section Title: Why Memory Is the Real Bottleneck for AI Agents — And What It Means for Multi-Agent Swarms
Content:
A parallel survey from ACM Transactions on Information Systems put it even more bluntly: “Memory is a key component that differentiates the agents from original LLMs, making an agent truly an agent.”
The latest research is moving fast. Here’s what’s on the frontier:
**Foundational architectures:**
→ **A-Mem** (NeurIPS 2025, arXiv:2502.12110) introduced Zettelkasten-inspired memory for agents — each memory unit is enriched with LLM-generated keywords, tags, and contextual descriptions, then dynamically linked to semantically related memories. The memory graph evolves as new knowledge arrives, retroactively refining existing notes. Think of it as a second brain that rewires itself.
Section Title: Why Memory Is the Real Bottleneck for AI Agents — And What It Means for Multi-Agent Swarms
Content:
→ **MemGPT / Letta** (arXiv:2310.08560) reframed memory as an operating system problem — agents that manage their own context window the way an OS manages virtual memory. The project has since spun out as Letta, backed by Felicis Ventures.
→ **Memoria** (arXiv:2512.12686) combines dynamic session-level summarization with weighted knowledge graphs, so agents incrementally build structured models of who you are — your traits, preferences, and behavioral patterns as entities and relationships.
→ **MIRIX** (arXiv:2507.07957, July 2025) went even further with a six-component memory system: Core, Episodic, Semantic, Procedural, Resource, and Knowledge Vault — achieving up to 26% relative improvement on LLM judge metrics.
→ **HippoRAG** (arXiv:2405.14831) drew directly from neuroscience, modeling the hippocampal indexing theory to create neurobiologically-inspired long-term memory for LLMs.
 ... 
Section Title: Why Memory Is the Real Bottleneck for AI Agents — And What It Means for Multi-Agent Swarms
Content:
The January 2026 paper “MAGMA: A Multi-Graph based Agentic Memory Architecture” attacks this directly — proposing multi-graph memory structures that can serve as shared context across agent swarms. Similarly, “EverMemOS: A Self-Organizing Memory Operating System” (January 2026) envisions memory as an OS-level concern for long-horizon multi-agent reasoning.
The research on multi-agent memory is still early but accelerating:
→ **G-Memory** (arXiv:2506.07398, June 2025) introduces hierarchical memory tracing for multi-agent systems — the first serious attempt at structured memory that spans agent boundaries.
→ **Intrinsic Memory Agents** (arXiv:2508.08997, August 2025) explores heterogeneous multi-agent LLM systems through structured contextual memory, where different agents maintain different types of memory that can be selectively shared.
Section Title: Why Memory Is the Real Bottleneck for AI Agents — And What It Means for Multi-Agent Swarms
Content:
→ **Memory Sharing for LLM-based Agents** (arXiv:2404.09982, May 2024) was among the first papers to directly address the question: when agents in a swarm learn something, how does that knowledge propagate to the others?
→ **RCR-Router** (arXiv:2508.04903) tackles role-aware context routing in multi-agent systems with structured memory — solving the problem of which agent gets which context.

results.title: Memory in LLM-based Multi-agent Systems
excerpts:
**Memory in LLM-based Multi-agent Systems:**
**Mechanisms, Challenges, and Collective**
**Intelligence**
**Shanglin Wu** 1 **and Kai Shu** 1
1 Emory University
**Memory plays a central role in transforming Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents from reactive**
**predictors into consistent, context-aware collaborators. While LLM-based single-agent memory has**
**been extensively studied, memory in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (LLM-MAS) lacks a systematic**
**taxonomy and review. In multi-agent contexts, memory becomes a shared cognitive infrastructure**
**enabling collective intelligence, long-term coordination, and team evolvement. This survey provides**
**the first comprehensive review of memory in LLM-MAS, synthesizing research across memory archi-**
**tectures, management and operations, evaluation, and application, while formalizing key definitions**
**and introducing the design space. Our survey reveals that memory in LLM-MAS is not a trivial ex-**
 ... 
support agents in both shared-goal and competitive scenarios. This scope reflects the current wave
of research that treats language agents as a new paradigm for AI coordination ( Gao and Zhang ,
2024 ; Rezazadeh et al. , 2025 ). The common theme is that enabling collective cognition via memory
which is a distinct challenge that sets multi-agent systems apart from single-agent ones. Overall, the
contributions of this survey are:
• Review the state-of-the-art in memory for LLM-MAS, summarize core concepts, and provide a
systematic taxonomy.
3
Memory in LLM-based Multi-agent Systems: Mechanisms, Challenges, and Collective Intelligence
• Critically compare memory architectures used in recent systems, integrating theoretical per-
spectives with practical considerations.
• Compile an extensive collection of relevant literature with concise summaries to serve as a
reference for researchers.
• Analyze key limitations of existing frameworks and propose comprehensive future directions.
 ... 
2025 ). These surveys collectively map out the LLM-MAS landscape, but memory is not their primary
focus. They mention memory as one mechanism among others, e.g. to maintain context or enable
self-evolution of agents, yet none provide a deep dive into memory architectures or systematically
compare how different frameworks implement memory. Our work differs by honing in on memory
as the lens for organizing and analyzing LLM-MAS literature, allowing us to bridge insights across
papers that a general MAS survey might treat only in passing.
**2.2. Memory for LLM Agents.**
Very recently, a dedicated survey on memory mechanisms for LLM-based agents was published by
Zhang et al. ( Zhang et al. , 2025d ). This survey recognizes memory as the key to enabling long-term,
complex interactions and attempts a first taxonomy of memory designs in LLM agents. Zhang et
al. discuss what memory is in the context of LLM agents and why it is needed, then review various

results.title: Agent Skills from the Perspective of Procedural Memory: A Survey - TechRxiv
excerpts:
AUTHOREA
Browse Preprints

Agent Skills from the Perspective of Procedural Memory: A Survey
Yaxiong Wu [](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1860-0122) ,
Yongyue Zhang
Yaxiong Wu
[](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1860-0122)
Independent Researcher
Corresponding Author: [[email protected]](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection)
Author Profile
Yongyue Zhang
Independent Researcher
Author Profile
Section Title: Abstract
Content:
As large language model (LLM)-powered autonomous agents increasingly demonstrate strong capabilities in complex real-world tasks, recent research has focused on leveraging skills to endow these agents with domain-specific expertise and adaptive competence. In this survey, we present the first systematic overview of LLM-powered Agent Skills from the perspective of procedural memory, conceptualizing skills as a core form of procedural knowledge that enables agents to internalize, retain, and reuse task-solving procedures through interaction and experience. We comprehensively examine what Agent Skills are, why they are essential for agentic intelligence, and how they can be effectively acquired, represented, invoked, and refined within LLM-based systems. Furthermore, we review representative applications across diverse domains and discuss key challenges and future opportunities in developing and generalizing Agent Skills.

Section Title: Artificial Intelligence & Deep Learning
Content:
Raymond de Lacaze Â· [December 20, 2025](https://www.facebook.com/groups/107107546348803/?multi_permalinks=2683494202043445&hoisted_section_header_type=recently_seen&__cft__%5B0%5D=AZaBTLeontKVJdxo-gVv5460rB9yzmIFJtuypkNNCR3RRqoWmvkB9ojo7wmHRlWfYlD3tlo27e4oP6621inxGMMbmjgjiO5jY9E8defWkcRkAtwmgNtJkzrTN8gILqExa4vizgsmYGVC4dbh9DizBcvduHguMaEdva534hkukgfeJp6x7LAurfY-HaMkcwkqqDRgCDpc9byvpZ6uOKItpH4U&__tn__=%2CO%2CP-R) Â·
Memory in the Age of AI Agents: A Survey (December 2025, 102 pages)
 ... 
Section Title: Artificial Intelligence & Deep Learning
Content:
"Memory has emerged, and will continue to remain, a core capability of foundation model-based agents. As research on agent memory rapidly expands and attracts unprecedented attention, the field has also become increasingly fragmented. Existing works that fall under the umbrella of agent memory often differ substantially in their motivations, implementations, and evaluation protocols, while the proliferation of loosely defined memory terminologies has further obscured conceptual clarity. Traditional taxonomies such as long/short-term memory have proven insufficient to capture the diversity of contemporary agent memory systems. This work aims to provide an up-to-date landscape of current agent memory research. We begin by clearly delineating the scope of agent memory and distinguishing it from related concepts such as LLM memory, retrieval augmented generation (RAG), and context engineering.
 ... 
Section Title: Artificial Intelligence & Deep Learning
Content:
We hope this survey serves not only as a reference for existing work, but also as a conceptual foundation for rethinking memory as a first-class primitive in the design of future agentic intelligence."

results.title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents What, When, How, and Where to Evolve on the Path to Artificial Super Intelligence
excerpts:
Section Title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents > 3 What to Evolve? > 3.2 Context > 3.2.1 Memory Evolution
Content:
LLM-based agents are increasingly designed with long-term memory mechanisms that grow and adapt as the agent continues to solve tasks and interacts with its environment (Shan et al. , [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib35 "Cognitive memory in large language models") ; Qian et al. , [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib50 "Experiential co-learning of software-developing agents") ) . An evolving memory enables the agent to accumulate knowledge, recall past events, and adjust its behavior based on experience. Many works stress that effective memory management is crucial for agent performance (Zhong et al. , [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib38 "Memorybank: enhancing large language models with long-term memory") ; Zhang et al. , [2025e](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib39 "G-memory: tracing hierarchical memory for multi-agent systems") ; Yan et al.
 ... 
Section Title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents > 3 What to Evolve? > 3.2 Context > 3.2.1 Memory Evolution
Content:
, [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib27 "Mem0: building production-ready ai agents with scalable long-term memory") ) introduces a two-phase pipeline where the agent first extracts salient facts from recent dialogue and then decides how to update the long-term memory: the agent can ADD new facts, MERGE/UPDATE redundant ones, or DELETE contradictions. Furthermore, Memory-R1 (Yan et al. , [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib449 "Memory-r1: enhancing large language model agents to manage and utilize memories via reinforcement learning") ) presents a reinforcement learning framework to train a dedicated Memory Manager agent that learns to select structured operations like ADD, UPDATE, and DELETE. Such a mechanism ensures the agent’s long-term memory is coherent and up-to-date. MemInsight (Salama et al.
Section Title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents > 3 What to Evolve? > 3.2 Context > 3.2.1 Memory Evolution
Content:
, [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib32 "Meminsight: autonomous memory augmentation for llm agents") ) augments raw memories with semantic structure, which summarizes and tags past interactions for retrieval later. REMEMBER (Zhang et al. , [2024a](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib22 "Large language models are semi-parametric reinforcement learning agents") ) combines an LLM with a memory of experiences and uses reinforcement learning signals to decide how to update that memory after each episode. Memento (Zhou et al. , [2025b](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.bib448 "Memento: fine-tuning llm agents without fine-tuning llms") ) enables continual adaptation without fine-tuning the LLM’s parameters by employing online reinforcement learning to optimize a case-retrieval policy, which allows the agent to learn from past experiences stored in an evolving memory bank.
 ... 
Section Title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents > References
Content:
Y. Cheng, W. Liu, K. Xu, W. Hou, Y. Ouyang, C. T. Leong, X. Wu, and Y. Zheng (2024) AutoPal: autonomous adaptation to users for personal ai companionship . arXiv preprint arXiv:2406.13960 . Cited by: [§8.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.SS1.p1.1 "8.1 Personalize AI Agents ‣ 8 Future Direction ‣ A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents What, When, How, and Where to Evolve on the Path to Artificial Super Intelligence") . P. Chhikara, D. Khant, S. Aryan, T. Singh, and D. Yadav (2025) Mem0: building production-ready ai agents with scalable long-term memory . arXiv preprint arXiv:2504.19413 . Cited by: [§3.2.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.SS2.SSS1.p1.1 "3.2.1 Memory Evolution ‣ 3.2 Context ‣ 3 What to Evolve? ‣ A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents What, When, How, and Where to Evolve on the Path to Artificial Super Intelligence") , [Table 2](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.21046v4.T2.65.63.63.10 "In 3.1 Models ‣ 3 What to Evolve?
